meta.title: The Whole Pet — Playable Case Study
meta.hubTagline: Build India's first integrated premium pet-wellness brand — daycare, boarding, grooming & vet-tech under one roof, Mumbai-first — in the white space no incumbent holds.

hero.sub: A playable walk-through of the Premium Pet Wellness dossier. Same facts, same numbers — but you get to investigate, decide, and vote.

opening.lead: No competitor in India owns premium physical locations, boarding/daycare, integrated health-tech and a subscription model at the same time. That single gap is the whole thesis. Here is the shape of it.

stats.labelHtml: Pets in India (2024) — the market this brand serves (EST.)
West India's share of India's pet-food revenue — Mumbai over-indexes on premium pet spend
Vetic clinics in Mumbai — the closest rival, but clinic-only: no daycare, no boarding
Competitors combining premium locations + boarding/daycare + health-tech + subscription — the whitespace, quantified

imgband.title: Three services, three strangers
imgband.sub: A neighbourhood groomer · an informal boarder found through a WhatsApp group · a vet clinic across town — with no shared record of vaccinations, allergies or visit history between any of them. The integrated middle is empty.

whitespace.lead: Eight real competitors, plotted. Hover any dot. Notice where nobody is: premium & experiential and full-stack integrated wellness. That corner is what this business is built to own.

players.d: 100+ stores, $75M; grooming is a counter, no health-tech, no boarding
+67% YoY, $63.8M; best tele-vet, zero physical locations
18yrs content trust, Ratan Tata; never scaled to services
65 clinics, 12 in Mumbai; clinic-only, no daycare/boarding
daycare ₹500-1,200/day; budget tier, no health records

whitespace.sidebarHtml:
Reading the map.

Supertails owns the best tele-vet but has no physical footprint. HUFT sits high on brand yet bolts grooming onto a shop. Vetic is the only player near the corner — proving vet+grooming+app at 12-clinic Mumbai density — but it is built and positioned as a clinic, not a premium daycare/boarding home.

The top-right is uncontested. That is the investable idea.

hook.titleHtml: Who actually owns the whole pet?
hook.lead: Six competitors were profiled in depth. Each built one piece of the puzzle — brand, tele-health, subscription, or clinical density — and none of them combined all four. Click each case file to see what they cover, and where the real gap is.
hook.statusInitialTail: open them all to close the case.
hook.statusSolvedHtml: Case closed. HUFT owns brand, Supertails owns tele-vet, Wiggles proved subscription, Vetic proved vet+grooming+app at 12-clinic Mumbai density — but not one of them has built premium daycare/boarding + integrated health-tech + subscription together. Vetic comes closest, yet reads as a clinic. The whitespace is real — and still open.

cases.name: HUFT
cases.det: 100+ stores, 80 grooming facilities, $75M raised (Sequoia/Peak XV, Verlinvest). But grooming is a counter bolted onto a shop, not a destination — and there is no health-record or tele-vet layer, no daycare, no boarding.

cases.name: Supertails
cases.det: Best digital/tele-vet product in the category, growing 67% YoY (₹64.6cr → ₹108.3cr), $63.8M raised. But zero physical locations — a customer who wants to drop a dog off for the day simply cannot use it.

cases.name: Wiggles
cases.det: Proved five years ago that pet owners will pay for a subscription box + vet-on-call line. But at ₹21.7cr revenue it has never achieved density anywhere — least of all Mumbai; it is Pune-rooted.

cases.name: Vetic
cases.det: 65 clinics, 11 cities, 12 in Mumbai — the strongest proof the hard part is fundable. But it is built and positioned as a clinic chain: no dedicated daycare or overnight boarding, functional rather than premium-experiential.

cases.name: The whitespace
cases.det: Premium physical locations + boarding/daycare + integrated health-tech + subscription — together. Vetic's density, HUFT's polish, Wiggles' subscription, plus the boarding none of them built well. That combination is uncontested.

personas.lead: Every persona has a trigger, an objection, and one thing that converts them. Flip each card to see what wins the sale.

items.name: Neha, 28–34
items.trig: 9–10 hour office-plus-commute days leave the dog alone; a guilt-driven search for trustworthy daytime care.
items.obj: Churns fast if a drop-off ever feels unsafe or the photo/video updates go quiet.
items.conv: Reliable daycare with photo/video proof of care + a flexible monthly membership (no per-day booking friction) + ideally an employer-subsidised wellness benefit.

items.name: The Malhotras
items.trig: Fragmented care today — grooming here, vet there, no single health record; anxious about breed-specific issues like hip dysplasia or skin conditions.
items.obj: Least price-sensitive of all, but demands genuine single-home integration, not a rebranded groomer.
items.conv: One trusted “wellness home” — grooming, vet visits, vaccination reminders, a digital health record and proactive AI-anomaly alerts. Top membership tier + retail upsell.

items.name: Rahul, 30–45
items.trig: Repeatedly needs trustworthy overnight boarding on short notice, especially across the Diwali/Christmas peaks.
items.obj: Been burned by informal arrangements; worries about kennel cough and peak-season availability.
items.conv: Guaranteed peak availability + live webcam/photo updates + vet-on-call backup + a frequent-boarder loyalty discount. Best fit for prepaid “boarding credits.”

items.name: Priya, 24–29
items.trig: Recently adopted her first pet (often a rescue/Indie dog or first cat); doesn't yet know what she doesn't know.
items.obj: Intimidated by traditional vet clinics; moderate income and a lower average ticket.
items.conv: An approachable, non-judgmental entry point — automatic vaccination reminders, an easy “is this normal?” tele-vet chat, affordable bundled guidance. High long-term value if “graduated” up; the key word-of-mouth persona.

tycoon.lead: Four real decisions from the dossier. Each has a right answer the diligence already argues for. Pick — then see what the evidence says.

decisions.q: 1 · Where do you plant the flag?
decisions.ctx: This is a capital-intensive real-estate business. Choose your rollout.

opts.t: Open flagships city-wide, fast — grab the corridors before rivals do
opts.fb: Per-flagship capex of ₹84 lakh–₹1.7 crore against premium Mumbai rents makes an all-flagship land-grab the fastest route to overextension. Speed here is a liability, not a moat.

opts.t: Pilot two flagships (Bandra/Khar + Powai), then hub-and-spoke
opts.fb: Correct. Prove unit economics over 12–18 months at 1–2 flagships, then scale via lower-capex ₹25–45 lakh satellites — mirroring Vetic's proven 12-clinic Mumbai pattern at a far better capex-to-revenue ratio.

decisions.q: 2 · How do you get the tele-vet layer?
decisions.ctx: Supertails has the best tele-vet product in India. Choose your path to that capability.

opts.t: Partner with or acquire Supertails for the tele-vet asset
opts.fb: A partnership doesn't solve your core gap (no physical locations), and an acquisition would import Supertails' documented login/OTP failures and payment-gateway issues open since May 2024 along with the asset.

opts.t: Build your own telehealth, informed by Supertails' visible mistakes
opts.fb: Correct. Building the layer yourself — with uptime, payment-flow QA and OTP robustness as first-class requirements — is the lower-risk path to the same capability, since the app is the core retention moat.

decisions.q: 3 · How do you price the grooming?
decisions.ctx: Review-mining surfaced a HUFT customer hit with an undisclosed extra ₹2,000 on a “full haircut.” Choose your pricing model.

opts.t: Premium pricing with flexible add-on charges at the counter
opts.fb: That is exactly the documented trust-killer — the surprise ₹2,000 upcharge that damaged HUFT's own reviews. It converts a premium into a reputation problem.

opts.t: Itemized, pre-committed, transparent quotes — no surprises
opts.fb: Correct. Radical pricing transparency directly answers the market's evidenced overcharge backlash and is a genuine, differentiated retention lever — not a nice-to-have.

decisions.q: 4 · How do you handle the GST ambiguity?
decisions.ctx: Grooming, boarding and daycare sit in a contested zone between nil-rated “animal husbandry services” and standard 18% GST.

opts.t: Assume the favourable nil-rate and price low to win share
opts.fb: A wrong classification carries retroactive liability. Betting the pricing on the optimistic reading is a tax exposure disguised as a discount.

opts.t: Get a formal advance ruling; price at a conservative 18%
opts.fb: Correct. Commission a GST advance ruling before finalizing pricing, price assuming the conservative 18% treatment, and treat any favourable ruling as upside — not as the business case.

human.titleHtml: The most unprotected pet in the room.
human.lead: The moat isn't just an app — it's a health record. Pet insurance penetration in India is below 1%, versus 25%+ in the UK and 40%+ in Sweden. Most pet spend here is out-of-pocket, which forces families into cost-based medical decisions for an animal they treat as family.
human.note: That sub-1% is inflecting — growing ~19–20% CAGR, with new entrants like Universal Sompo and Future Generali writing ~25,000 policies in a single quarter. A continuously-updated pet health record positions this chain as a future insurer-distribution partner — turning the data moat into real financial protection for a family's pet, not just a retention hook.

quiz.lead: Five questions. Then you cast the vote you'd cast in the room.

opts: Nobody has ever groomed a dog in Mumbai
No competitor combines premium physical locations + boarding/daycare + integrated health-tech + subscription at once
Pet ownership is illegal without a licence
The government funds premium pet chains
items.e: HUFT owns brand/retail, Supertails owns tele-vet, Wiggles proved subscription, Vetic proved vet+grooming+app density — but none of the four has combined all the pieces. That absence is the thesis.

items.q: Which competitor comes closest — and why does it stop short?
opts: HUFT — because its grooming is a store counter
Supertails — because it has zero physical locations
Vetic — 65 clinics, 12 in Mumbai, but it is a clinic with no daycare/boarding
DogSpot — because it never converted content to services
items.e: Vetic is the single most important read: it proves vet+grooming+app is fundable at real Mumbai density, but it is positioned as a clinic — no dedicated daycare or overnight boarding, and a functional rather than premium-experiential brand.

items.q: What is the estimated Year-5 Mumbai revenue (SOM)?
opts: ₹4–8 crore/year (EST.)
₹500 crore/year
₹4–8 lakh/year
₹100 crore in Year 1
items.e: A deliberately conservative, bottom-up Mumbai SOM of roughly ₹4–8 crore/year by Year 5 (EST.) — built from the organized pet-services sub-segment, not the $25bn headline pet-economy number.

items.q: What is the single highest-severity risk the dossier names?
opts: The Instagram algorithm changing
A single animal-safety incident — injury, escape or a fight — carrying outsized, viral, brand-wide reputational risk
Running out of grooming shampoo
Too few competitors to benchmark against
items.e: Animal safety/liability is treated as the top operational risk: one incident at any one location is socially viral and hits the whole brand — which is why intake screening, staff-to-animal ratios, CCTV and insurance are non-negotiable.

items.q: Why should Aamara run this rather than buy a proven pet business?
opts: Because pet care has guaranteed returns
Because the proven integrated version doesn't exist yet — the whitespace IS the opportunity, and the window closes if HUFT, Vetic or Supertails add the missing piece first
Because animal care is tax-free
Because vets work for free
items.e: None of the funded players has combined the four pieces — but the gap won't stay open: HUFT could add an app, Vetic could add boarding, Supertails could open stores. Moving first, in Vetic's own proven Mumbai geography, is the argument for now.

vote.prompt: You've seen the whitespace, the diligence on every incumbent, the personas, the unit economics and the human number. What's your call?
vote.yesMsg: Backed. Your Phase-1 first move (per the Action Plan): commission a GST advance ruling before any pricing is finalized, shortlist 4–6 Bandra/Khar & Powai sites for landlord LOIs, and scope the health-record app with a reliability requirement informed by Supertails' documented failures — all inside the first 30 days. Then prove unit economics at 1–2 flagships over 12–18 months before any wider Mumbai, Maharashtra and India rollout.
vote.noMsg: Passed — and defensibly so. The dossier agrees the variance is real: a capital-intensive real-estate model with 5–15% mature net margins, membership churn unproven at scale in India (Wiggles never disclosed its), a single animal-safety incident carrying viral brand-wide risk, and well-funded incumbents (HUFT $75M, Vetic's 65 clinics, Supertails +67% YoY) who could close the whitespace faster than a standing start. A pass here is a legitimate IC position.

footer.note1: This playable walk-through is a companion to the full institutional dossier — it simplifies for engagement and never overrides the source document.
footer.note2: Every number here is drawn from the dossier, including its EST. flags and unverified-source caveats.
